text: Functional and Non-Functional Requirements

This section establishes what TesTly must do (its functional requirements) and how well it must do it (its non-functional requirements). The functional requirements are captured as **user stories** — short, value-oriented statements written from the perspective of a system actor — grouped by actor in Sections 4.1.1 to 4.1.3, consolidated into a prioritised backlog in Section 4.1.4, and complemented by the quality attributes set out in Section 4.1.5. Each story follows the canonical agile template, "As a `<role>`, I want `<goal>` so that `<benefit>`", which forces every requirement to name not only the action a user performs but also the reason it matters to that user. This keeps the requirement grounded in a real need rather than a technical convenience, and it provides a natural unit of work for incremental development and for acceptance testing later in the project.

TesTly recognises three human actors, modelled by the `Role` enumeration in the database (`Patient`, `LabStaff`, `Admin`), plus a non-human AI assistant that acts on the patient's behalf. The stories below are grouped by actor. They are deliberately written at a consistent level of granularity — each maps to a coherent slice of functionality that can be exercised end-to-end — and each group corresponds to one of the use-case diagrams presented alongside it. Because the admin console is web-only by design, while patients and lab staff are served by both the web and mobile clients, the actor groupings also implicitly describe the cross-platform reach of each capability.

Taken together these requirements describe one continuous journey. Before enumerating the stories actor by actor, Figure 4.1 traces that journey for the platform's primary actor — the patient — as an activity workflow that runs from anonymous discovery through cross-lab comparison, booking, and payment to result delivery and longitudinal trend tracking. The lab-side and administrative workflows that support each step are detailed later in this chapter.

caption: The end-to-end patient journey workflow — from anonymous discovery and cross-lab price comparison, through booking, payment, and the chosen collection mode, to result delivery and cross-lab health-trend tracking.

text: Patient User Stories

The patient is the primary consumer of the marketplace and therefore has the richest set of stories, spanning anonymous discovery, booking and payment, results consumption, longitudinal health tracking, and conversational assistance. A defining characteristic captured in these stories is that discovery requires no account: the landing page, lab search, lab detail, and test-comparison views are all unauthenticated, and only labs that an administrator has activated are ever exposed. The complete patient actor model is summarised in the patient use-case diagram.

caption: Patient use-case diagram — anonymous browsing, comparison, booking and payment, results, cross-lab trends, reviews, and AI assistance.

items: As a **patient**, I want to browse and search the catalogue of accredited labs without first creating an account so that I can evaluate the platform before committing to register.

As a **patient**, I want to filter and sort labs by free-text query, city, price, rating, distance, accreditation, and home-collection capability so that I can quickly narrow a fragmented market to the options that fit my needs.

As a **patient**, I want to compare the price of a single named test (such as a CBC) across every lab that offers it so that I can find the most affordable accredited provider for the exact test I need.
As a **patient**, I want labs to be ranked by how near they are to my current location so that I can prefer a convenient nearby lab over a distant one.
As a **patient**, I want to book a test as a lab visit, a home sample collection, or a mail-in home test kit so that I can choose the collection mode that suits my circumstances.
As a **patient**, I want to choose a future appointment slot (or receive an estimated kit-delivery date) so that the booking fits my schedule and the slot is reserved for me.
As a **patient**, I want to pay online or settle in cash in a manner appropriate to my booking type so that I can use whichever payment method I trust and have available.

As a **patient**, I want to track each booking through its lifecycle, see a status timeline and kit-shipment progress, and cancel a pending or confirmed booking so that I always know the state of my order and can change my mind.

As a **patient**, I want to receive my official result as a downloadable PDF together with a plain-language summary so that I can both keep the authoritative document and understand my result at a glance.
As a **patient**, I want to view longitudinal charts of my health markers spanning results from different labs so that I can observe trends over time even when I switch providers.
As a **patient**, I want to control whether my history is shared only with the lab I am currently using or across labs I have booked with so that I retain ownership of my sensitive medical data.
As a **patient**, I want to leave a rating and review after a completed booking so that I can share my experience and help other patients choose a lab.

As a **patient**, I want to ask an AI assistant where to get a test and what it means, and have it surface clickable lab and test cards, so that I receive trustworthy guidance grounded in the real catalogue.
As a **patient**, I want to receive push notifications when a booking is confirmed or rejected, a kit is shipped, or a result is ready so that I do not have to keep checking the app.

text: Lab Staff User Stories

Lab staff operate a complete back-office storefront, but only after an administrator has activated their account. The stories therefore begin with a gated registration and proceed through catalogue and schedule management, booking processing, results delivery, and a privacy-scoped view of patient history. The lab actor model is shown in the lab use-case diagram.

caption: Lab Staff use-case diagram — gated onboarding, catalogue and schedule management, booking processing, result upload and delivery, and privacy-scoped patient context.

items: As a **lab staff member**, I want to register my lab and complete an onboarding profile so that I can be reviewed by an administrator and admitted to the marketplace.
As a **lab staff member**, I want a workspace dashboard that aggregates my booking queue, catalogue, schedule, and analytics so that I can run my operations from a single screen.

As a **lab staff member**, I want to create, update, and retire tests with their own price, description, preparation, and turnaround so that my catalogue reflects exactly what my lab offers and at what price.
As a **lab staff member**, I want to publish and manage future appointment slots so that patients can book only when my lab is actually available.
As a **lab staff member**, I want to confirm or reject pending bookings — confirming online bookings only once payment has cleared — so that I commit my capacity only to valid, paid orders.

As a **lab staff member**, I want to record cash collected and advance the home-test-kit fulfilment through its shipment stages so that the order state stays accurate for the patient and for my records.
As a **lab staff member**, I want to toggle whether my lab offers home collection and home test kits so that I expose only the collection modes I can actually service.

As a **lab staff member**, I want to upload the official result PDF, attach a summary and highlights, and add structured analyte values so that the patient receives an authoritative document and contributes to cross-lab trends.

As a **lab staff member**, I want to view a patient's clinical history, bounded by that patient's privacy setting and never exposing another lab's raw PDF, so that I can prepare and interpret a sample responsibly.
As a **lab staff member**, I want to see my own reviews and be notified of new bookings and reviews so that I can monitor demand and reputation.

text: Administrator User Stories

The administrator is the platform's curator and governor. Because TesTly is a curated marketplace rather than open self-service, the administrator's stories centre on lab governance — verifying that a lab is operationally complete before it becomes bookable — together with platform-wide analytics and user oversight. The admin actor model is shown in the admin use-case diagram.

caption: Administrator use-case diagram — lab governance with onboarding-readiness gating, platform analytics, user management, and payments oversight.

items: As an **administrator**, I want a governance dashboard listing every lab with its onboarding status and an explicit readiness breakdown so that I can see at a glance which labs are ready for approval and which requirements they still lack.

As an **administrator**, I want to activate, reject, or suspend a lab — with activation refused for any lab that fails the readiness gate — so that every bookable lab in the marketplace is guaranteed to be operationally complete.

As an **administrator**, I want to view platform statistics and analytics, including booking volume, revenue by city, and the most popular tests, so that I can understand and steer the health of the marketplace.
As an **administrator**, I want a searchable list of patients with their booking counts so that I can support and oversee the user base.
As an **administrator**, I want a feed of recent payments across the platform so that I can monitor financial activity and investigate issues.

text: Representative User-Story Backlog

The following table consolidates a representative subset of the backlog into a single prioritised view. Priorities follow the **MoSCoW** convention (Must, Should, Could): "Must" items constitute the minimum viable marketplace, "Should" items add the differentiating value of TesTly, and "Could" items are valuable enhancements. The identifiers are referenced again in later chapters when mapping requirements to implementation and tests.

caption: Representative prioritised user-story backlog across the three actors.
rows: Browse and search labs without registering
Compare one named test across all labs by price
Rank labs by distance from my location
Book a test as lab visit, home collection, or home test kit
Pay online or by an appropriate cash method
Track booking status and cancel a pending/confirmed booking
Receive a result PDF plus a plain-language summary
View cross-lab health-marker trends over time
Review a lab after a completed booking
Chat with an AI assistant grounded in the catalogue
Register a lab and complete onboarding for review
Manage the test catalogue and pricing
Confirm/reject bookings and record cash collected
Upload result PDF and add structured observations
Review labs with an onboarding-readiness breakdown
Activate/reject/suspend labs with a readiness gate
View platform analytics and recent payments

text: Non-Functional Requirements

Beyond the functional behaviour captured by the user stories, TesTly is bound by a set of **non-functional requirements** (NFRs) — the quality attributes that a system handling medical records and money must satisfy regardless of any single feature. These were treated as design constraints from the outset rather than as afterthoughts, and each is realised by concrete mechanisms elsewhere in this chapter. Table 4.2 states each attribute, the requirement it imposes, and the means by which the implementation meets it. The stated availability and response-time figures are engineering targets for the platform under normal load rather than measured production service levels.

caption: Non-functional requirements and how the TesTly implementation addresses each
rows: Medical and payment data must be protected in transit and at rest, and credentials must resist theft and replay.
bcrypt-hashed passwords; 8-hour access JWT plus SHA-256-hashed, rotating refresh tokens; `httpOnly`+`SameSite` cookie (web) and OS secure-keystore Bearer token (mobile); HTTPS in production.
Each actor may access only the resources its role permits.
Layered guard chain — `JwtAuthGuard` → `RolesGuard` → `LabActiveGuard` → per-service ownership checks — backed by fast edge-middleware routing on the web.
Patients must control whether their medical history is visible across competing labs.
`LabHistorySharing` defaults to the most restrictive `SAME_LAB_ONLY`; enforced at the data-access layer; a lab's raw PDF is never exposed to another lab.
No double-booking, and financial state must never be lost or corrupted.
ACID transactions; a database `@unique` constraint on `schedule_slot_id`; integer-EGP money fields that avoid floating-point error.
Interactive reads should respond quickly under normal load (target ≈ 1–2 s).

Denormalised rating aggregates; indexes on hot filter columns (city, rating, scheduled time); single aggregated workspace queries via `Promise.all`; pagination; token-by-token SSE streaming for AI replies.
The platform must scale horizontally as labs and patients grow.
Stateless JWT API instances; serverless, branchable Neon PostgreSQL with pooled connections; pluggable S3-compatible object storage.
The core service must stay usable even when optional integrations fail (target ≥ 99% core API).
Graceful degradation (object storage → local disk, push → no-op, AI → service-unavailable) and a Prisma retry middleware for serverless cold-starts.
Both clients must be consistent, responsive, and accessible.
Shared design tokens; responsive layouts and dark mode; semantic labels; chart information encoded by colour *and* text, never colour alone.
The platform must serve a bilingual (Arabic/English) Egyptian audience.
Full mobile English/Arabic localisation with automatic RTL (330 keys per locale); the AI assistant replies in the user's language.
A small team must be able to evolve the system safely.
End-to-end TypeScript/Dart type safety; a single `schema.prisma` source of truth with versioned migrations; modular NestJS; conventional-commit history.
Security- and money-relevant actions must be traceable for accountability.
A structured audit logger plus an append-only `BookingStatusEvent` trail recording who performed each transition.

text: User Interface Design

The user-interface design of TesTly was developed concurrently across the two client platforms — a Next.js web application and a Flutter mobile application — over a single shared backend API. The two clients deliberately mirror one another: the mobile palette is ported directly from the web application's design tokens, the two share an identical distance formula, and the same search, booking, results, and trends flows appear on both. This section presents the key screens of the implemented interface, illustrated with screenshots captured from the running web client against the seeded demonstration data; the Flutter mobile client mirrors these layouts natively, as noted throughout. The design language is consistent across every screen: white rounded cards on a light neutral background, a blue brand accent, pill-shaped tab bars, and a clear visual hierarchy of header, primary content, and call-to-action.

Two cross-cutting properties hold for every screen. First, the interface is **responsive**: the web application uses a CSS-token design system (Tailwind 4) with fluid card grids and sticky headers that adapt from desktop to small viewports, while the mobile application is natively laid out for phone form factors with bottom-navigation shells. Second, the design supports **dark mode**: a dark theme variant is declared in the web stylesheet and is the default behaviour of the error and loading boundaries, while the mobile application ships a full Material 3 dark theme selectable as System, Light, or Dark in the profile screen. The mobile client additionally provides full English/Arabic localisation with automatic right-to-left layout for Arabic.
Patient Home and Search

The patient's entry point is a landing/home screen built around discovery. On the web, a sticky header carries the brand mark, anchor links, and a role-aware sign-in or dashboard control. Beneath it sits a blue-gradient hero containing a trust badge, a large headline, and a two-field **search bar** (test or symptom, plus an optional city), followed by popular-search chips (CBC, Lipid Profile, Thyroid Panel, HbA1c) and "browse by" shortcuts for Nearest, Best Price, and Top Rated. Below the hero are a feature grid, a featured-labs section populated by the top-rated labs, a four-step "How It Works" strip, and a footer. Submitting a search composes a query string and navigates to the comparison screen; selecting a featured lab opens its detail page. The mobile home screen mirrors this exactly as a native layout — a gradient hero greeting the user by first name, a tappable search field, the same browse-by and popular-search chips, a horizontally scrolling featured-labs carousel, and the feature and how-it-works sections — with a floating action button that opens the AI assistant.

The search/comparison screen presents two pills toggling between a **Tests** view and a **Labs** view. The Tests view shows a grid of aggregated test cards (name, category, "from EGP X", and "available at N labs"). The Labs view shows a sort-and-filter bar — a Filters control with an active-count badge, a home-collection toggle, and a sort selector for Price, Rating, or Distance — above a column of lab cards. An expandable advanced-filter panel exposes range sliders for minimum rating, maximum distance, and maximum price, plus accreditation toggle chips. Each lab card shows the lab name, rating, distance, address, accreditation, turnaround, an optional home-collection tag, the relevant price, and a **Book Now** action. To support the Distance sort, the client requests the browser (or device) geolocation and, when granted, ranks results by great-circle distance.

caption: The implemented web landing page — the hero search (test or symptom plus optional city), popular-test shortcuts, and Nearest / Best Price / Top Rated browse links (web client).
Search and comparison results — the Tests/Labs toggle, filter and price-sort controls, and lab cards showing price, rating, accreditation, and turnaround (web client).

text: Lab Detail and Test Comparison

The lab detail screen opens with a header card naming the lab and showing its rating and review count, distance, and address, followed by a grid of key facts (accreditation, turnaround time, contact phone, and email) and an optional home-collection indicator. A patient-reviews card lists published reviews with reviewer initials, star ratings, comments, and dates, with reviewer names privacy-reduced to a first-name-and-initial form. The screen's main body is the **Available Tests** catalogue, filterable by category chips, where each test card presents the name, price, description, preparation, turnaround, and parameter count, and a *Book This Test* button that carries the chosen lab and test into the booking flow. The mobile twin renders the same content within a collapsing app bar and a pinned two-tab bar separating Tests from Reviews.

The complementary test-comparison screen answers the inverse question — "who offers this test, and at what price?". It leads with a test-information card (name, category, description, a facts row, and a parsed preparation checklist), then a sort-and-filter bar offering Nearest, Price, and Rating ordering, and finally a list of every lab offering that test. Each entry shows the lab, a distance badge, rating, accreditation, turnaround, the price, home-collection and home-test-kit tags, and *View Lab* and *Book Now* actions. Distance here is computed client-side from each offer's coordinates, so the list re-sorts to Nearest as soon as a location fix arrives.

caption: Lab detail page — header facts (rating, address, accreditation, turnaround, contact) above the category-filterable test catalogue with per-test price and a 'Book This Test' action (web client).
Cross-lab test comparison — every lab offering the selected test, ranked by price, with rating, accreditation, home-collection tags, and 'View Lab' / 'Book Now' (web client).

text: Booking Flow

The booking flow is a guided, multi-step wizard reached only by authenticated patients. It opens with a lab-information card (lab, address, selected test, and base price) and proceeds through a sequence of clearly separated decisions, each rendered as a card on the web and as a discrete step on mobile:

items: **Choose collection type** — three large selectable tiles for Visit Lab, Home Collection (+EGP 100), and Home Test Kit (+EGP 150); the home options are disabled unless the lab offers them.

**Select a slot** — for lab-visit and home-collection bookings, a date grid and a time-slot grid populated from available future slots; for the kit flow, an explanatory delivery banner replaces the slot picker.
**Provide an address** — a home-address field appears for the home-collection and kit flows.
**Choose payment** — a Pay-online (demo) option or a context-appropriate cash option (cash at lab, cash to collector, or cash on delivery) matched to the booking type.

**Review and confirm** — a booking-summary card itemises the test price plus any collection or kit fee to a total, restates the payment method, and presents the Confirm Booking (or Order Kit) action; on success a confirmation card is shown and the patient is returned to the dashboard.

text: Because a slot is reserved atomically when the booking is created, the interface gracefully handles the case where a slot is claimed concurrently: the conflict surfaces as a toast and availability is re-fetched so the patient can pick again. The mobile booking flow implements the identical sequence as an explicit step state machine and, on success, additionally schedules a local preparation reminder the evening before a test that requires fasting.

caption: The guided booking flow — collection-type tiles (lab visit, home collection, home test kit, the last two disabled when the lab does not offer them), date and time-slot selection, and the simulated payment step (web client).

text: Patient Dashboard — Bookings, Results, and Trends

The patient dashboard is the authenticated home for managing activity. A header shows the patient's avatar, name, and role pill alongside actions for the AI Assistant, browsing labs, and logout, with three quick-stat tiles for total bookings, upcoming bookings, and results. The body is organised by a four-tab pill bar:

items: **Bookings** — one card per booking with a status-coloured left border, the test and lab, a status badge, a grid of date/type/total/address, a kit-status progress bar for kit orders, a payment line, demo-payment controls for pending online payments, and a cancel action for pending or confirmed bookings.

**Results** — one card per result showing its status, a "structured data · N values" or "PDF only" badge, an AI summary block of highlighted key/value items, an *Open PDF* link, and a review widget (a five-star picker and comment) shown only when the result is reviewable and not yet reviewed.
**Trends** — the longitudinal health-trends panel (described below).
**Profile** — editable name, phone, and address, a read-only email, and the lab-history-sharing selector with an explanatory privacy note.

text: The trends panel renders one line chart per analyte series, plotting normalised values over time. A shaded reference band marks the most recently reported reference interval, and individual data points are coloured to flag abnormal versus in-range readings. Each chart carries date-range chips (3, 6, or 12 months, or all), grouping chips (by analyte or by lab test), a textual point-by-point breakdown, a trend badge (rising, falling, stable, or not enough points), an Export-CSV action, and a standing medical disclaimer. On mobile the same panel is drawn with the `fl_chart` library, including dashed reference lines and a recent-readings table; on the web it uses the Recharts library. Crucially, because the underlying observations are normalised to canonical units, a single chart can combine results from different labs.

caption: Patient dashboard — quick-stat tiles and the Bookings tab, each booking card showing status, type, total, payment state, and the relevant actions (web client).

Cross-lab health trends — a normalised marker (fasting glucose) charted over time with a shaded reference band, an abnormal-point flag, and a rising-trend narrative, combining results issued by different labs (web client).

text: AI Assistant Chat

The AI assistant is presented as a familiar chat interface: a header with an assistant avatar and a *New chat* control, a scrollable message area, and a bottom composer with an auto-sizing multi-line input and a send button. An empty state greets the patient by name and offers suggestion chips (for example, "How do I prepare for a fasting blood test?"). User messages render as blue bubbles and assistant messages as neutral bubbles, with an animated typing indicator while a reply streams in. The assistant's distinguishing feature is **agentic tool-result cards**: when it looks up the catalogue it attaches clickable lab cards and test cards inline, each deep-linking into the corresponding lab-detail or test-comparison screen, which turns the conversation into an on-ramp to the booking funnel. A persistent disclaimer beneath the composer reinforces that the assistant does not provide a diagnosis. This agentic assistant is distinct from the lightweight guided-FAQ chatbot that floats on every web page for cheap, common questions.

caption: The agentic Health Assistant — a personalised greeting, suggested starter questions, the streaming chat composer, and a standing medical-safety disclaimer (web client).

text: Lab Dashboard — Schedule, Catalogue, and Results

The lab workspace is organised under a multi-tab bar covering Bookings, Tests, Results, Schedule, Analytics, Reviews, and Settings. The Bookings tab lists pending bookings first and presents each with the patient, test, date, status, a details grid, a payment line, and the appropriate actions — Confirm and Reject (with Confirm disabled until an online booking is paid), a record-cash-received control for cash bookings, the home-test-kit action ladder (ship with tracking, mark delivered, mark sample received), and a button that opens the privacy-scoped patient-context drawer. The Tests tab combines an add-test form with inline-editable test rows offering edit, delete, and active-toggle. The Schedule tab pairs an add-slot form (start, end, and capacity) with a list of slots that can be deactivated. The Results tab, for confirmed or completed bookings, provides a PDF file picker and summary field with an upload action, a mark-delivered action, and access to the patient-context drawer. The Analytics tab presents stat cards (total bookings, completed, pending results, revenue estimate, capacity-usage percentage, and top tests), while Reviews lists the lab's published reviews and Settings exposes the home-collection and home-test-kit capability toggles.

The patient-context drawer is a right-hand overlay that renders only what the patient's privacy setting permits: a privacy notice, the patient's sharing setting and the lab's effective scope, demographics, recurring-test signals, a twelve-month trend summary, and prior bookings — with cross-lab entries clearly flagged as belonging to another lab and PDF links available only for this lab's own uploads.

caption: Lab workspace — the Bookings queue (with Confirm / Reject, cash recording, and the patient-context drawer) under the Bookings / Tests / Results / Schedule / Analytics / Reviews / Settings tab bar (web client).

text: Admin Dashboard — Pending Labs and Transactions

The admin dashboard, available only on the web, opens with a gradient header and a stats row (total labs, active labs, total bookings, platform revenue, and a highlighted pending-approvals count). Its content is organised into four tabs. The **Lab Approvals** tab — carrying a pending-count badge — offers a search box, status-filter chips, and lab cards; pending labs are visually tinted, and each card exposes an Approve action (disabled unless the lab's onboarding readiness is satisfied), a Reject action, and a View-Details expander revealing a stat grid and an onboarding-readiness summary that names exactly which requirements are still missing. The **User Management** tab presents a searchable patient table; the **All Bookings** tab shows a table of the most recent payments (booking, patient, lab, test, date, status, and amount); and the **Analytics** tab renders the platform charts — a booking-volume area chart over thirty days, a revenue-by-city bar chart, and a most-popular-tests bar chart — together with a client-aggregated top-performing-labs list.

caption: Admin governance dashboard — platform statistics (total/active labs, bookings, revenue, pending approvals) and lab-approval cards with onboarding status and approve/reject actions (web client).

text: Mobile Client (Flutter Application)

Although the screenshots above are taken from the web client, every patient-facing flow is also delivered as a native mobile application built with Flutter and compiled from a single Dart codebase to both iOS and Android. The mobile client is not a web view: it renders native widgets, ports its colour and spacing tokens directly from the web application's design system, and consumes the same NestJS API documented in Section 4.3. Figure 4.15 shows the iOS build running on the simulator against the same seeded demonstration data — role-based login, the personalised home screen, a lab's detail and catalogue, the bookings list, and the cross-lab health-trends chart (drawn natively with the fl_chart library) — confirming the cross-platform parity referred to throughout this section.

caption: The TesTly mobile client (Flutter), running on the iOS simulator. Top row, from left: role-based login, the personalised home screen, and a lab's detail with its test catalogue. Bottom row: the patient's bookings list and the cross-lab health-trends chart with reference bands and abnormal-value flagging. The mobile UI mirrors the web client's design tokens and calls the identical backend API.

text: System Architecture and Database Design

This section describes how TesTly is structured internally: its layered runtime architecture, the modular organisation of the backend, the high-level flow of data through the system, the relational database schema with its distinctive structured-results design, the REST API surface, and the authentication and authorisation model that protects the medical and financial data the platform handles.
Layered Runtime Architecture

TesTly is delivered as an npm-workspaces monorepo containing three applications over a single API contract: a NestJS backend, a Next.js web application, and a Flutter mobile application. Architecturally the system is layered. At the top, two thin **client** layers — the web SPA-plus-storefront and the native mobile app — render the user interface and hold no business logic. Both consume one **API** layer, the NestJS server, which is the sole holder of business rules and the only component that touches the database. Beneath the API sit a **persistence** layer (PostgreSQL accessed through the Prisma ORM) and a set of **external service** integrations (object storage for result PDFs, Firebase Cloud Messaging for push, and Google Gemini for the AI assistant). The overall layering and the placement of each external dependency are shown in the architecture diagram.

caption: Layered system architecture — two clients over one NestJS API, backed by PostgreSQL and three external services.

text: A deliberate theme of the architecture is that the optional external services **degrade gracefully** when they are not configured: object storage falls back to local disk, push notifications become a no-op, and the chat endpoint returns a service-unavailable response. This lets the core API run in any environment. The deployment topology that realises this architecture — the web app on Vercel, the API on Railway (or Render), and the database on a managed Postgres provider — is presented in the deployment diagram.

caption: Deployment topology — Vercel-hosted web app, Railway/Render-hosted API, managed PostgreSQL, and the external S3, FCM, and Gemini services.

text: Backend Module Structure

The NestJS backend is decomposed into feature modules under `apps/backend/src/`, each owning a cohesive slice of the domain: authentication and authorisation; the public marketplace; bookings and payments; the patient and lab workspaces (including results handling); clinical normalisation; administration; the FAQ helper; the AI chat assistant; and notifications. Cross-cutting concerns — a global validation pipe, a standardised exception filter, audit logging, and a Prisma retry middleware for serverless-database resilience — are applied uniformly across the modules. The module layout and the request pipeline are shown in the backend component diagram.

caption: Backend module/component diagram — feature modules and the layered guard pipeline.

text: A distinctive feature of the request pipeline is its **layered guard chain**. Every protected request passes, in order, through a `JwtAuthGuard` that proves identity, a `RolesGuard` that enforces the required actor type, a `LabActiveGuard` that confirms a lab account has been activated, and finally a per-service ownership check that verifies the caller actually owns the resource being acted upon. No single guard is trusted to be sufficient; together they implement defence in depth appropriate to a system handling medical data and money.
High-Level Data Flow

At a high level, a request originates in one of the two clients, carrying authentication credentials — an HTTP-only JWT cookie for the web app or a Bearer token for the mobile app. It reaches the NestJS API, where it traverses the validation pipe and the guard chain before entering a feature service. The service applies business rules, reads and writes the database through Prisma, optionally invokes an external service (persisting a PDF, sending a push notification, or calling the language model), and returns a uniform, camel-cased response — or, for the AI assistant, a stream of Server-Sent Events. The end-to-end movement of data through these stages is depicted in the data-flow diagram.

caption: High-level data-flow diagram — client request through validation, guards, and services to the database and external integrations.

text: Database Schema and the Structured-Results Design

The persistence layer is a PostgreSQL database accessed through Prisma, comprising nineteen tables (models) and eleven enumerations. Primary keys are opaque UUIDs, monetary amounts are stored as integer Egyptian pounds in fields suffixed `_egp`, and column names use snake_case with standardised `created_at`/`updated_at` timestamps. The schema groups into six functional clusters — identity and accounts, catalogue and scheduling, transactions, results, trust/content/auth plumbing, and the AI assistant — and has evolved through ten migrations. The full entity-relationship model is shown in the ERD.

caption: Entity-relationship diagram — the nineteen models with Booking as the central hub and the structured-results marker sub-graph.

text: The identity cluster centres on a single `User` table with a `role` discriminator, extended by optional one-to-one `PatientProfile` and `LabProfile` records; this keeps authentication uniform while pushing role-specific data into profile tables. `LabProfile` is the lab's storefront record, carrying location, capability flags, an onboarding status, and denormalised `rating_average`/`review_count` aggregates for fast listings. Tests are modelled **per-lab** (`LabTest`), so the same conceptual test exists as many rows each with its own price — the basis of the comparison marketplace — while `LabScheduleSlot` represents bookable time windows.

The `Booking` table is the hub of the schema. It references the three parties to an order (patient, lab, and test), optionally claims one schedule slot through a unique foreign key that guarantees a slot is held by at most one active booking, and carries three parallel concerns in one row: the order itself, a payment block (method, status, reference, and timestamped failure/clearance fields), and a kit-logistics block for mail-in kits. Each booking owns an append-only `BookingStatusEvent` trail that records how and by whom every status transition occurred, giving both a fast current-state read and a queryable audit history.

Results are stored in **two complementary forms**, which is the most architecturally interesting aspect of the schema. The legally authoritative document is the `ResultFile` (one PDF per booking, served only through an authorisation-checked proxy), accompanied by a human-readable `ResultSummary`. Alongside these sit the **structured-results stack**, whose purpose is to let a patient and authorised labs trend a marker — say fasting glucose — across results from *different* labs, even though every lab names and units its analytes differently. Four tables collaborate: a `ResultPanel` groups observations under a booking; each `ResultObservation` stores both the raw reading (name, value, unit, and reference range exactly as reported) **and** a canonical form (a converted `value_in_canonical_unit`, a `comparable_unit`, an `is_comparable` flag, and an explanatory note). The canonical anchor is the `CanonicalMarker` table — a single internal identity per analyte, with a stable code, a default canonical unit, and an optional LOINC code — and `MarkerAlias` maps the many normalised raw spellings a lab might print onto that one marker. Resolution proceeds from an explicit code, to an alias match, to a fuzzy display-name match; values whose unit cannot be recognised are explicitly flagged non-comparable so they never pollute a shared trend line, and clinical-precision decimals are used throughout. This canonical-marker design is what makes cross-lab trending possible without ever exposing one lab's raw document to another.
The schema's eleven enumerations encode the controlled vocabularies of the domain. The principal ones are listed below.

caption: Principal database enumerations and their meaning.
rows: Admin moderation state of a lab; only Active labs can transact
Review moderation; only Published feeds ratings

text: REST API Design

The backend exposes a REST API of roughly fifty endpoints, documented automatically via an OpenAPI/Swagger UI. The design is conventional and uniform: resources are addressed by noun-based paths, the HTTP verb expresses the operation (GET to read, POST to create, PATCH to partially update, PUT to replace, DELETE to remove), request bodies are validated declaratively by data-transfer objects, and every response — whether success or error — follows a consistent shape, with errors wrapped in a standardised envelope. Endpoints are grouped by module: an unauthenticated public marketplace, the authentication endpoints, the bookings and payments routes, the patient and lab workspaces, the results routes, the admin governance routes, the FAQ helper, and the AI chat endpoints. A representative selection of the most important endpoints is shown below.

caption: Representative selection of key REST API endpoints.
rows: Register a lab account in PendingReview
Authenticate and issue an access/refresh token pair
Search, filter, sort, and paginate active labs
List every lab offering a named test, price-ascending
Open future slots for a lab and test
Lab confirms or rejects a pending booking
Upload the official result PDF and summary
Download a result PDF through an authorisation check
Activate, reject, or suspend a lab
Send an AI-assistant message; reply streamed over SSE

text: Authentication and Role-Based Access Control

Authentication serves two client transports from one codebase. On login the API issues a short-lived (8-hour) access JWT and a long-lived (30-day) refresh token. For the web client the access token is delivered as an HTTP-only cookie — which, in production, is made first-party by a same-origin `/api` rewrite so the cookie survives the split deployment — while the mobile client receives the token in the response body and stores it in the device's secure keystore, sending it as a Bearer header. The server's JWT strategy accepts either transport via two extractors. Refresh tokens are opaque random secrets of which only a SHA-256 hash is stored, and they are **rotated on use**: each refresh revokes the consumed token and issues a fresh pair, limiting the damage a stolen token could do. Passwords are stored only as bcrypt hashes.
Authorisation is enforced as a layered chain, illustrated in the authentication sequence for lab onboarding.

caption: Lab onboarding and activation sequence — gated registration, readiness check, and admin approval.

items: **Identity** — the `JwtAuthGuard` validates the token and re-fetches the user from the database, so a token belonging to a deleted user is rejected.
**Role** — the `RolesGuard` compares the authenticated user's role against the roles declared on the route, admitting only the correct actor type.

**Lab activation** — the `LabActiveGuard` additionally requires that a lab-staff user's account has been moved to the Active onboarding status, blocking pending, rejected, or suspended labs from operating even though they can authenticate.
**Ownership** — finally, each service performs an ownership check (for example, a lab may act only on its own bookings), a defence-in-depth layer that does not rely on the route guard alone.

text: On the web client this server-side model is complemented by a fast Edge-middleware guard that decodes the JWT to route users to the correct dashboards and redirect inactive labs, while the API itself remains the authoritative gate. The result is a consistent, defence-in-depth access-control posture spanning both clients and every protected route.
